topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice sat on my desk, crisp white paper mocking the grime of my cramped dorm room. 'Due to declining enrollment and fiscal restructuring, the institution will cease operations in the spring.' I didn't even bother reading the fine print. I just crumpled the letter, the sound sharp and satisfying in the silence of the empty apartment. Every carefully curated memory of late-night study sessions, of the camaraderie I’d tried to build, felt suddenly tainted, like cheap plastic. I slammed my fist onto the desk, the cheap veneer groaning under the impact. I stood up, pacing the narrow confines of the room, my jaw tight. It wasn't sadness I felt; it was a cold, hard resentment that settled deep in my chest, a burning desire to watch everything crumble around this pointless endeavor.

[story 2]

"So, that's it then?" I asked, my voice flat, devoid of any inflection. I stared at the brochure for the upcoming orientation, the glossy pages suddenly looking like propaganda. The sheer audacity of it—this place, this supposed launchpad for my future, just dissolving into dust. I picked up a discarded textbook, its spine cracked, and tossed it against the wall with unnecessary force. A tightness gripped my throat, a pressure that made breathing shallow. I wanted to scream at the administration, to point out the absurdity, but the words caught, lodged somewhere behind a wall of pure, simmering annoyance. I kicked at a loose floorboard, the cheap wood splintering under my boot, channeling the churning feeling into physical motion.

[story 3]

She watched the final announcement scroll across the screen, her expression utterly blank. The lights in the common area seemed too bright, exposing the emptiness of the space. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped her lips, heavy and reluctant. She didn't move to gather her things; there was no need. The weight of expectation, the entire structure she had invested in, was simply collapsing. A sharp, bitter taste flooded her mouth. She walked over to the window, not to look outside, but to stare at the brick façade of the building, feeling a profound sense of being cheated. Her shoulders slumped, a visible surrender to the disappointment, and she let out a low, almost inaudible sound—a sound that held no sorrow, only a deep, simmering dissatisfaction with the entire situation.
